description: This figure presents a mechanical property map plotting total elongation (TE) versus ultimate tensile strength (UTS) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels alongside conventional steel grades, using uniform ASTM E-8 specimen geometry (50 mm by 12.5 mm by 1 mm). The tensile data were compiled from solution-treated and water-quenched strips reported in multiple literature sources. The scatter plot categorizes steels into six microstructural regimes represented by colored ellipses: δ single phase (including IF, Mild, BH, CMn, HSLA), δ-based duplex, TRIP/DP/CP, γ single phase (Mn-TWIP), γ-based duplex, and martensitic steels (MS) containing γ+(δ)+coarse κ. The x-axis spans 0–1800 MPa for UTS, the y-axis spans 0–100% for TE, and a top axis indicates the strength-ductility product (UTS×TE) in GPa%, with diagonal contour lines marking constant product values. Mn-TWIP steels occupy the upper-right region with UTS ~600–1000 MPa and TE ~60–95%, while conventional δ single-phase ferritic steels cluster in the lower-left with moderate strength and ductility. MS grades exhibit high strength (>1000 MPa) but poor ductility (<10%). Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys primarily distribute within the γ single-phase and γ-based duplex fields, demonstrating that austenitic lightweight steels achieve exceptional strength-ductility balances competitive with conventional advanced high-strength steels.
